sections.question: Whether as the legal advisor for the city's planning commission, you had a disqualifying interest under the Political Reform Act in a planning commission decision concerning a conditional use permit for a water well located approximately 1500 feet from your residence.
sections.conclusion: On the basis of your representations, you were advised that it did not appear that a disqualifying conflict was present, given that: (1) any impact on the fair market value of the residence was speculative; and (2) whether the water service would be substantially improved was similarly speculative since the new well would supplement rather than replace the existing well and it was unknown whether the water quality would be higher.
sections.question_synthetic: Does a city attorney serving as legal advisor to the planning commission have a disqualifying conflict of interest under the Political Reform Act when voting on a conditional use permit for a water well located 1500 feet from her residence?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC advised that no disqualifying conflict appears to exist based on the requestor's representations that impacts on property value and service improvements were speculative rather than concrete.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document is confirmation of prior telephone advice. Question and conclusion sections identified and extracted verbatim. Document dated April 16, 1991, File No. I-91-174. Clear structure with advice letter format.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Whether as the legal advisor for the city's planning commission, you had a disqualifying interest under the Political Reform Act in a planning commission decision concerning a conditional use permit for a water well located approximately 1500 feet from your residence.

CONCLUSION: On the basis of your representations, you were advised that it did not appear that a disqualifying conflict was present, given that: (1) any impact on the fair market value of the residence was speculative; and (2) whether the water service would be substantially improved was similarly speculative since the new well would supplement rather than replace the existing well and it was unknown whether the water quality would be higher.

embedding.summary: This advice letter confirms telephone guidance provided to the Deputy City Attorney of Fresno regarding potential disqualifying conflicts of interest under the Political Reform Act concerning a planning commission decision on a water well conditional use permit near her residence. The FPPC concluded no disqualifying conflict was present based on the speculative nature of impacts to property value and service improvements.
